name: Capstone — your AI operating plan
tagline: No new ideas — just the assembly. Everything the last eight modules built becomes two shipped things: one automation running end to end, and a one-page plan that says what's live, what's next, the rules it runs under, and when you'll look at it again. That plan is what makes you AI-operational.
overview: This is the module where it stops being a course and starts being how you run. There is no new theory here — the whole job is to take what you already made and pin it down on one page. You know what AI is and isn't (Module 00), you found where it fits (01), you can brief it well (02), it knows your business from your own documents (03), one real workflow now runs without you (04), inside guardrails you chose (05), under a short policy your team has read (06), and you know what it costs and what it frees up (07). The capstone assembles those into a one-page AI operating plan with four short sections — Running (the automation that's live, who owns it, what it costs, when it's reviewed), Next (the two candidates you circled on your opportunity map, in order, each with a start date), Rules (your one-page policy, pointed at, not rewritten), and Review (the 15 minutes a month you spend keeping it honest). You'll learn how that monthly review actually goes — open the cost tab, make one scale-it / keep-it / kill-it call, promote one thing from Next to Running, sanity-check the policy — and the three ways month three quietly goes wrong (the novelty fades and the review gets skipped; the plan swells to four pages; a shiny new tool tempts a from-scratch restart). Then the honest send-off: where you go from here if you want to go further, named without a sales pitch — deeper platform mastery, a real business credential, and the builder's lane for anyone on the team who caught the bug. You finish by shipping the five-step capstone checklist and writing the plan itself. The point of the one page is not tidiness. It's that operating plans which actually get used are always short.
objectives: Assemble the eight modules' artifacts into a one-page AI operating plan with four sections — Running, Next, Rules, and Review — that fits on a single page on purpose.
Write the Running section from a real automation: what's live, who owns it, what it costs this month, and its review rhythm, grounded in the platform's cost visibility and activity log.
Run the monthly 15-minute review as a fixed habit — read the cost tab, make one scale/keep/kill call, promote one candidate, and sanity-check the policy.
Name the three month-three failure modes (the review gets skipped, the plan bloats, a new tool tempts a restart) and the one-line fix for each.
State honestly what 'AI-operational' means — a running system, a written policy, a review habit, and the judgment to know what stays human — and where the growth paths lead without being sold to.

lessons.title: The one-page operating plan: Running, Next, Rules, Review
lessons.objective: Assemble the artifacts you already built into a single-page plan with four sections, and understand why each section is there and why it stays short.
lessons.body:
## You already did the hard part

Here's the good news up front: there is nothing new to learn in this module. Every piece of the operating plan already exists — you made it, one module at a time. The capstone is just assembly. If a course ever felt like it was building toward something, this is the something.

Walk the chain in one breath. You know what AI actually is and where it breaks (Module 00). You mapped where it fits your business (01). You can write a clear brief instead of a vague wish (02). It knows *your* products and policies because you uploaded your own documents (03). One real workflow now runs without you babysitting it (04). It runs inside guardrails you chose — scope, approval, budget, a written record (05). There's a one-page policy your team has actually read (06). And you know what it costs you and what it frees up (07).

Those are your artifacts. The operating plan is the single page that ties them together so you — and anyone else in the business — can see the whole thing at a glance.

## Four sections, one page

The plan has exactly four parts. Keep the whole thing to one page; the constraint is the feature, not a limitation.

1. **Running** — what is live *right now*. The automation you shipped in Modules 04 and 05, named plainly, with three facts beside it: who owns it (a real person, usually you), roughly what it costs this month, and how often it's reviewed. If it isn't running, it doesn't go here — this section is reality, not intention.
2. **Next** — the two candidates you circled on your opportunity map back in Module 01, in priority order, each with a start date. Not ten ideas. Two. A date turns a wish into a plan.
3. **Rules** — your one-page policy from Module 06. You don't rewrite it here; you *point at it*. One line: "We operate under our AI policy — see [wherever it lives]." The rules already exist; the plan just references them so nobody forgets they're there.
4. **Review** — the standing habit. One line naming when you look at all of this again: "15 minutes, first Monday of the month." The next lesson is entirely about what happens in those 15 minutes.

That's the whole plan. Running / Next / Rules / Review. If yours is longer than a page, you're describing, not operating — cut it back.

## Why short wins

A plan you have to *find* and *read* is a plan you'll skip. The one-pager works precisely because it's glanceable: you can pull it up in the monthly review, see everything, make a couple of calls, and close it in a quarter of an hour. Operating plans that people actually use are always short — the moment this becomes a four-page document, it becomes a document nobody opens. Length is how good intentions die.

## Worked example — the e-commerce shop's finished page

A small online homeware shop finishes the course. Here's their operating plan, read off the page:

> **RUNNING** — *Weekly review-reply drafter.* Every Monday, the assistant pulls the week's new customer reviews, drafts a reply to each, and leaves them for the owner to approve and post. Owner: me. Cost: about £6 last month (visible on the cost tab). Review: monthly.
>
> **NEXT** — (1) *Returns-email first-drafts* — start next month. (2) *Restock-reminder summaries from supplier emails* — start when returns is steady.
>
> **RULES** — We operate under our one-page AI policy (uploaded to our knowledge base). Nothing customer-facing goes out without a human approving it.
>
> **REVIEW** — 15 minutes, first Monday monthly: check the cost tab, decide scale/keep/kill on the drafter, promote one item from Next, re-read the policy's approval line.

Notice what that page does *not* contain: no strategy essay, no roadmap deck, no jargon. One automation that's genuinely live, two things queued with dates, a pointer to the rules, and a review habit. The whole business's AI operation, on a page you could read at a bus stop. That is the deliverable — and building it is the last thing this course asks of you.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What are the four sections of the one-page AI operating plan?

options.text: Running, Next, Rules, Review
Strategy, Budget, Roadmap, Risks
Goals, Tools, Team, Timeline
Prompt, Model, Cost, Report

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The plan is deliberately just four short sections: **Running** (what's live now), **Next** (the two queued candidates with dates), **Rules** (a pointer to your policy), and **Review** (the monthly 15 minutes). The other options are plausible-sounding business headings, but they're not this plan — a strategy/roadmap framing (b) invites the four-page bloat the plan exists to prevent, (c) and (d) are just lists of things you already covered in earlier modules, not the operating structure.

knowledgeCheck.stem: An automation you're *planning* to build but haven't shipped yet — which section does it belong in?

options.text: Next — Running is only for automations that are actually live
Running — anything you intend to build counts as running
Rules — because it changes how the business operates
It goes in all four sections so nothing is missed

knowledgeCheck.explanation: **Running is reality, not intention** — only automations that are genuinely live go there, which is what keeps the plan honest. A planned build is a candidate, so it belongs in **Next**, ideally with a start date. Putting intentions in Running (b) turns the plan into wishful thinking; Rules (c) is where your policy lives, not your backlog; and spreading one item across all four sections (d) is exactly the bloat the one-page format is designed to prevent.

lessons.title: The monthly 15 minutes — and how month three goes wrong
lessons.objective: Run the monthly review as a fixed habit using the platform's own cost and activity surfaces, and recognise the three ways the habit quietly fails by month three, with the fix for each.
lessons.body:
## The review is the whole point

A plan you write once and never open is decoration. What makes you *operational* rather than merely *set up* is the habit of looking again. So the plan's fourth section — Review — earns its own lesson, because this is the part everyone underestimates.

The review is 15 minutes a month. Not an hour, not a meeting. Put it on the calendar as a fixed slot — "AI review, first Monday" — because a review that depends on you remembering is a review that stops happening. Calendared beats intended.

## What actually happens in the 15 minutes

Four moves, in order:

1. **Read the cost tab.** Open your workspace's cost view — the platform tracks every model call and shows you total cost, cost per request, and which agent or model is your **Top Spender** for the month. You're looking for one thing: any surprise. A number that's roughly what you expected means carry on. A number that jumped means investigate before it compounds.
2. **Make one scale / keep / kill call on what's Running.** For your live automation, decide one of three things. *Scale it* — it's earning its keep, so widen the lane or run it more often. *Keep it* — it's fine, leave it alone. *Kill it* — it's not paying for the attention it costs, so switch it off. Most months the answer is "keep," and that's a perfectly good answer. The discipline is *making the call*, not changing something for the sake of it.
3. **Promote one candidate from Next to Running.** If the live automation is steady, take the top item from your Next list and start it. One at a time — you're widening the lane as trust earns it, exactly the graduated approach from Module 05, not launching five things at once.
4. **Sanity-check the policy.** Thirty seconds: is the approval line from your Module 06 policy still being honoured? Is anything customer-facing slipping out without a human check? Re-read the one line that matters and move on.

That's it. Cost, one call, one promotion, one policy glance. Close the plan. You've kept the whole operation honest in less time than a coffee break.

## How you actually see cost and activity

Two surfaces carry this review, and both are already in your workspace. The **cost tab** shows spend down to the individual request, broken out by model and by agent, with a projected monthly estimate and a flag on unusual spikes — that's your "any surprises?" check. And the **activity feed** is the running log of what your agents actually did: every action and tool call, in order, so you can read it back like a manager doing a five-minute skim of the week. You looked at both in earlier modules; the review is just the habit of returning to them on a schedule. One honest caveat: the platform shows you the *cost* half of the picture — it does not compute "hours saved" or an ROI figure for you. The time-saved side of the story is yours to estimate, the way you did on your Module 07 one-pager.

## Month three: the three quiet failures

The course ends; month three is where the habit is really tested. Three things go wrong, predictably, and each has a one-line fix.

**1. The novelty fades and the review gets skipped.** By month three the shine is gone and "I'll do the AI review next week" becomes a permanent state. This is the big one — a plan without a review decays into a screenshot of a good intention. *Fix:* calendar it as a recurring 15-minute slot with your name on it. If it's on the calendar, it happens; if it depends on remembering, it doesn't.

**2. The plan grows to four pages.** You add a note here, a sub-goal there, a paragraph of context, and the one-pager quietly becomes a document. Now it's something you file, not something you glance at — and a plan you don't glance at is a plan you don't use. *Fix:* cut it back to one page at every review. If it won't fit, something isn't really Running or Next — it's noise.

**3. A shiny new tool tempts a restart.** A new AI product launches, the demo is dazzling, and the pull to tear everything down and rebuild on it is strong. *Fix:* remember the plan outlives the tool. Your operating plan is about *what runs in your business and under what rules* — it does not care which vendor's logo is behind it. A better tool is a line-item you might swap in during a review, not a reason to start over. The workflow, the guardrails, and the review habit are the asset; the tool is replaceable.

## Worked example — the marketing agency's third-month review

A small marketing agency runs a live automation that drafts weekly client-update emails for a project lead to approve. Month three, first Monday, 15 minutes:

- **Cost tab:** £22 this month, up from £14. The Top Spender is the drafting agent — expected, because they onboarded two new clients, so no alarm. Noted, not panicked.
- **Scale / keep / kill:** *Keep.* It's saving the lead a chunk of Friday and the clients haven't complained. Leave it.
- **Promote one:** the top Next item — *first-draft replies to inbound proposal enquiries* — gets a start date for next week.
- **Policy glance:** approval line intact; every client email still goes out only after a human signs off. Good.

Notice the failure modes they *dodged*: the review happened because it was on the calendar (not skipped), the plan is still one page (they resisted adding a "lessons learned" appendix), and when a colleague pitched a flashy new writing tool mid-month, they parked it as a maybe-swap for a future review instead of restarting. Fifteen minutes, and the operation stayed honest for another month.

knowledgeCheck.stem: During the monthly review, what is the FIRST thing you check to catch a cost problem early?

options.text: The cost tab — total spend, cost per request, and the Top Spender for the month
A built-in ROI dashboard that shows hours saved versus money spent
The number of new AI tools released since last month
How many pages your operating plan has grown to

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The review opens on the **cost tab**, which tracks spend per request and flags the Top Spender — your one-glance 'any surprises?' check before a small overspend compounds. Option (b) is a trap: the platform shows you cost, but there is no built-in 'hours saved' or ROI figure — that half is your own estimate. New tool releases (c) are a distraction the review is meant to resist, and page count (d) is worth a glance but isn't your early-warning signal for *cost*.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A dazzling new AI tool launches in month three and you're tempted to rebuild everything on it. What's the right move, per the failure-mode fixes?

options.text: Park it as a possible swap to weigh at a future review — the plan outlives the tool
Tear down the current automation and rebuild on the new tool immediately
Add three pages to the plan documenting the new tool's features
Cancel the monthly review until the new tool is fully evaluated

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Your operating plan is about *what runs and under what rules*, not which vendor is behind it, so a shiny tool is a **line-item swap to consider at a review**, not a reason to restart. Rebuilding on impulse (b) throws away the working workflow, guardrails, and habit that are the real asset; padding the plan with feature notes (c) triggers the bloat failure mode; and pausing the review (d) triggers the worst failure mode of all — the review that quietly stops happening.

lessons.title: What 'AI-operational' means — and the roads from here
lessons.objective: Define completion as AI-operational (a running system, a written policy, a review habit, and the judgment to keep some things human), and name the honest growth paths from here without a sales pitch.
lessons.body:
## You're not 'doing AI' — you're running a slightly better business

Let's be honest about what you've achieved, because it's easy to either undersell it or oversell it, and both are wrong.

You are not an AI expert. That was never the goal, and anyone who tells you a short course made you one is selling something. What you *are* is **AI-operational** — a specific, real thing, and it has four parts:

1. **A running system.** One real workflow in your business genuinely runs without you doing it by hand. Not a demo, not a someday — a thing that happened this week, on its own, inside limits you set.
2. **A written policy.** A one-page set of rules your team has read, so that when AI touches customers or money, everyone knows what's allowed and what needs a human first.
3. **A review habit.** Fifteen minutes a month keeping the whole thing honest — cost checked, one call made, the policy still holding.
4. **The judgment to know what stays human.** Maybe the most valuable part. You know what AI is genuinely good at, where it makes things up, and which decisions in *your* business you would never hand to it. Knowing when *not* to use AI is not a gap in your knowledge — it's the mark of someone who actually understands the tool.

That combination — system, policy, habit, judgment — is the certificate that matters. There may be a completion badge you can share if it's useful to you, but the badge isn't the achievement. The one-page plan pinned up in your business is.

## The honest 'when not to' still stands

Nothing in this capstone walks back the caution from earlier modules. AI still makes confident mistakes; that's a designed-around property of how these tools work, not a scandal you can patch away — it's exactly why your policy has human-approval points and why some tasks stay off the list entirely. Being AI-operational includes keeping that list. The business owner who automates the routine drafting and still reads every customer reply before it sends hasn't failed to go far enough — they've understood the tool correctly.

## Where the roads go from here

If the capstone is enough — one workflow running, a plan you'll review — you're done, and that's a complete and legitimate place to stop. But some people finish this and want more. Three roads lead onward, and none of them is a thing you have to buy to have gotten your money's worth here.

- **Go deeper on the platform.** If a workflow outgrows this course — you want several agents coordinating, richer automations, more of what the platform can do — there's a fuller platform-mastery track that picks up where the capstone stops. It's the next door over for the operator who caught the bug.
- **Get a real business credential.** If you want something externally recognised — a genuine 'prove it' — there's a business-audience AI credential (the Gen-AI-Leader exam) aimed squarely at leaders rather than engineers. It's the honest upgrade if a certificate on the wall would help you.
- **Learn to build.** If someone on your team turned out to *enjoy* this — the wiring, the guardrails, the making-it-work — there's a practitioner lane for people who want to build these systems properly, not just operate them.

Those are doors, not a checkout page. You walked in wanting to understand this thing everyone was selling you and to make it useful in your business. You did both. Review the plan monthly, widen the lane as trust earns it, and let the tools come and go underneath a plan that outlasts them.

## Worked example — the consultancy's send-off

A two-person consultancy finishes the course. Their live automation summarises the week's client calls into a draft status note each partner reviews. Are they 'AI experts'? No — and when a prospect asks, they say so plainly. But they're operational: the summariser runs every Friday, their one-page policy says client-identifying details get a human check before anything leaves, they do the 15-minute review on the first Monday, and they've drawn a hard line that AI never sends a client email unread. One partner is curious enough to look at the deeper platform track next quarter; the other is happy exactly where they are. Both are right. That's what finished looks like — not a finish line you cross into expertise, but a better way of working that you now simply *have*.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What does being 'AI-operational' actually mean at the end of this course?

options.text: A running system, a written policy, a review habit, and the judgment to know what stays human
Becoming an expert who can build AI models from scratch
Automating every task in the business with no human involvement
Buying the most advanced AI tools on the market

knowledgeCheck.explanation: AI-operational is a specific, modest, real thing: **one workflow that runs, a policy in writing, a monthly review habit, and the judgment to keep some decisions human.** It is explicitly *not* becoming an expert (b) — the course never claimed to make you one. It is not automating everything (c); knowing what to keep human is part of the definition. And it has nothing to do with owning the fanciest tools (d) — the plan outlives whichever tool you use.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You finish the course and want an externally recognised business credential — a genuine 'prove it'. Which road is aimed at that?

options.text: A business-audience AI credential (the Gen-AI-Leader exam), aimed at leaders rather than engineers
Nothing — there is no path onward once you finish this course
The deeper platform-mastery track, which is a credential you can put on a CV
The builder's practitioner lane, which is the only option for anyone who finishes

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The honest 'prove it' upgrade is the **Gen-AI-Leader** business credential — a real, externally recognised exam pitched at leaders. There are absolutely paths onward, so (b) is wrong. The deeper platform track (c) builds skill but isn't the external *credential* described — it's the 'go deeper' door. And the practitioner lane (d) is for people who want to *build* systems, not the only option and not aimed at credentials — the whole point is that these are different doors for different goals.

questions.stem: What is the AI operating plan meant to fit on?

options.text: One page — the short length is the point, because a plan you glance at is one you'll actually use
A detailed multi-chapter document covering every scenario
A slide deck for quarterly board presentations
Whatever length it naturally grows to over time

questions.explanation: The plan is deliberately **one page** — short enough to pull up and read in the monthly review. A multi-chapter document (b) or a board deck (c) becomes something you file and forget, which is how the habit dies. Letting it grow to any length (d) is literally one of the month-three failure modes; you cut it back to one page at every review.

questions.stem: In the plan, what goes in the 'Running' section?

options.text: The automation that is genuinely live now, with its owner, rough monthly cost, and review rhythm
Every idea you might build someday
The full text of your AI policy
A record of every chat message you've sent to an agent

questions.explanation: **Running is reality** — only what's actually live, named plainly, with who owns it, what it costs, and when it's reviewed. Ideas you might build (b) go in **Next**. The policy text (c) lives in **Rules**, which the plan points at rather than restating. And a chat transcript (d) isn't part of the operating plan at all.

questions.stem: How does the 'Rules' section handle your Module 06 AI policy?

options.text: It points at the policy with one line, rather than rewriting it into the plan
It copies the full policy text into the operating plan
It replaces the policy — the plan is now the only rules document
It deletes the policy, since the plan makes it redundant

questions.explanation: Rules is a **pointer**: one line saying 'we operate under our AI policy — see [where it lives].' Copying the whole policy in (b) blows the one-page budget and duplicates a document you already have. The plan doesn't replace (c) or delete (d) the policy — the policy stays the authority; the plan just references it so nobody forgets the guardrails exist.

questions.stem: How many candidates should the 'Next' section hold, and what turns each from a wish into a plan?

options.text: Two, in priority order, each with a start date
As many ideas as you can think of, unordered
One, with no date — dates make the plan too rigid
Ten, so you never run out of things to build

questions.explanation: **Two** candidates, in order, each with a **start date** — the date is what makes it a plan instead of a wish. A long unordered list (b) or ten items (d) is a backlog that overwhelms rather than guides, and you'd only ever promote one at a time anyway. One item with no date (c) drops the very thing that makes Next actionable; keeping two also means you always have the next promotion ready.

questions.stem: The monthly review takes about 15 minutes. Which set of moves matches it?

options.text: Read the cost tab, make one scale/keep/kill call, promote one candidate, sanity-check the policy
Rewrite the entire plan from scratch each month
Launch every candidate on the Next list at once
Hold an hour-long team meeting to debate strategy

questions.explanation: The 15 minutes is four moves: **cost check, one scale/keep/kill call, one promotion, one policy glance.** Rewriting the plan monthly (b) is wasted effort and invites bloat. Launching everything at once (c) breaks the graduated 'widen the lane as trust earns it' discipline — you promote one. And an hour-long strategy meeting (d) is exactly what the lightweight review is designed to avoid; keep it short so it actually happens.

questions.stem: Which is the single most common way the whole operating plan quietly fails by month three?

options.text: The novelty fades and the monthly review gets skipped
The automation runs too reliably and needs no attention
The cost tab stops tracking spend after 90 days
The team reads the policy too often

questions.explanation: The biggest month-three failure is the **review getting skipped** once the novelty wears off — a plan without a review decays into a good intention, and the fix is a recurring calendar slot. An automation running reliably (b) is success, not failure. The cost tab doesn't expire (c) — that's invented. And a team engaging with the policy (d) is the opposite of a problem. Calendar the review and this failure mode is defused.

questions.stem: Your operating plan has quietly grown to four pages. What's the fix, and why does it matter?

options.text: Cut it back to one page — a plan you don't glance at is a plan you don't use
Keep all four pages; more detail always means a better plan
Split it into four separate documents, one per page
Move it into a slide deck so the length looks intentional

questions.explanation: **Cut it back to one page** at every review — if it won't fit, something isn't really Running or Next and should be trimmed. More detail (b) is precisely the trap: a document you file instead of glance at stops getting used. Splitting it into four documents (c) or dressing it up as slides (d) just hides the bloat rather than removing it — the value is in the single glanceable page.

questions.stem: The review's 'read the cost tab' step tells you what you spent. What can the platform NOT hand you automatically?

options.text: A ready-made 'hours saved' or ROI figure — the time-saved side is your own estimate
The total cost for the period
The cost broken down by agent or model
A projected monthly cost estimate

questions.explanation: The cost tab gives you total spend, per-agent/per-model breakdowns, and a monthly projection — but there is **no built-in 'hours saved' or ROI metric**; you supply the time-saved half yourself, as you did on your Module 07 one-pager. So (a) is the thing it can't hand you; (b), (c), and (d) are all things the cost view genuinely provides. Knowing this keeps you honest: don't wait for a value dashboard that doesn't exist.

questions.stem: The capstone checklist asks that one person besides you can run and check the automation. On a workspace, what makes that achievable?

options.text: Roles — you can add a teammate as a Member (who can run playbooks and check activity) so the automation isn't solely yours
Nothing — automations can only ever be run by the single person who created them
You must give the teammate full Admin so they can do anything at all
The automation runs itself with no human able to check it

questions.explanation: Workspaces have three roles — Admin, Member, Viewer — and a **Member** can run playbooks and view activity and reports, which is exactly enough for a second person to run and check the automation. So (b) is false; work isn't locked to its creator. You don't need to hand over full **Admin** (c) — that also unlocks settings, billing, and credentials the teammate doesn't need for this. And an automation nobody can check (d) contradicts the whole point of the 'one person besides you' step.

questions.stem: For the 'Running' section you want to show the automation has run and record where its actions are visible. Which platform surface is that?

options.text: The activity feed — the chronological log of every action and tool call the agents made
The marketplace, where you publish the automation for others
The voice-chat panel
The document upload page

questions.explanation: The **activity feed** is the running log of what your agents actually did — every action and tool call in order — so it's where you confirm the automation ran and can point to its work. The marketplace (b) is for sharing agents and playbooks, not viewing run history; voice chat (c) and document upload (d) are unrelated surfaces. Reading the feed like a manager's weekly skim is the visibility habit the review depends on.

questions.stem: The plan can help draft itself. What's the honest way to describe that?

options.text: Ask your grounded assistant to draft the one-pager from your own artifacts, then edit it until it's true
The assistant writes a perfect final plan you can publish unread
You must write every word by hand; AI can't help with the plan
The platform generates the plan automatically with no input from you

questions.explanation: Since your assistant is grounded in your own documents (Module 03), it can **draft** the one-pager from your map, policy, and cost sheet — and then you **edit it to true**. Publishing it unread (b) ignores everything the course taught about human-checking AI output. It's not true that AI can't help (c) — drafting is a good use. And the platform doesn't conjure the plan from nothing (d); it drafts from *your* artifacts and *your* review, with you making the final call.

questions.stem: A colleague argues: 'Being AI-operational means the business runs itself now — no more human judgment needed.' What's the correction?

options.text: AI-operational explicitly includes the judgment to know what stays human; keeping some decisions off the AI is part of the definition, not a shortfall
They're right — a good operating plan removes the need for any human decisions
They're right, but only until the next tool upgrade
AI-operational means you've become an AI expert who no longer needs guardrails

questions.explanation: AI-operational is four things — a running system, a written policy, a review habit, and the **judgment to know what stays human** — so 'no human judgment needed' gets the definition backwards. Removing all human decisions (b) contradicts the policy's approval points and the honest 'when not to use AI' stance. A tool upgrade (c) changes nothing about that judgment. And it was never about becoming an expert who drops guardrails (d) — the guardrails and the human line are the point, not training wheels you outgrow.

labs.title: Ship it: the five-step capstone checklist and your one-page plan
labs.goal: Turn eight modules of work into two finished things: your Module 04 automation running end to end under Module 05's guardrails, and a one-page AI operating plan (Running / Next / Rules / Review) drafted by your grounded assistant and edited to true. The artifact is the operating plan itself, plus a live automation with at least three runs behind it and a calendared monthly review. This is the deliverable that makes you AI-operational.
labs.steps:
## Before you start

You'll do this on your own free Automatos workspace, where you're the admin of your personal workspace (it's created for you on first sign-in). Nothing new gets built here — you're assembling and pinning down what earlier modules produced. Have your artifacts to hand: your opportunity map (Module 01), your one-page policy (06), and your cost/ROI one-pager (07). If any are missing, that's your signal to loop back before finishing.

## The five steps

**1. Running — confirm the automation is genuinely live.** Open your Module 04 automation and check it has actually run at least three times (on its schedule if it's a scheduled one). Confirm Module 05's guardrails are in place: the working agent's tool scope is no wider than the job needs, and anything customer-facing or money-out still requires your approval. Then open the **cost tab** and note this month's spend for it — that number goes on the plan. If it hasn't run three times yet, let it run and come back; Running means *running*.

**2. The plan — draft it from your own artifacts, then edit to true.** In chat, ask your grounded assistant (the one that knows your business from your uploaded documents) to draft a one-page AI operating plan from your map, policy, and cost sheet, using the four headings **Running / Next / Rules / Review**. Then edit its draft until every line is actually true of your business — the assistant drafts, you decide. Under **Next**, put the two candidates you circled on your opportunity map, in order, each with a start date. Under **Rules**, add one line pointing at where your policy lives (for example, uploaded to your knowledge base). Keep the whole thing to one page.

**3. The team — share the policy and set roles to match.** Make sure your one-page policy is where the team can read it. In **Team Management** (admin-only, which on your own workspace is you), check that roles fit the plan: if a teammate needs to run and check the automation, a **Member** role lets them run playbooks and view activity without handing over full admin. Aim for the checklist condition: one person besides you can run and check the automation.

**4. The review — put a real 15 minutes on the calendar.** Create a recurring monthly calendar slot titled 'AI review' — first Monday, 15 minutes. Write the agenda straight into the Review section of your plan: read the cost tab, make one scale/keep/kill call, promote one candidate from Next, sanity-check the policy's approval line. A review that lives on the calendar happens; one that lives in your head doesn't.

**5. Claim it.** You're now AI-operational — a running system, a written policy, a review habit, and a clear sense of what stays human. If a completion badge is available and useful to you, take it; but the real certificate is the one-page plan itself. Pin it somewhere you'll see it.

## Why it matters

The course was always building toward this page. A running automation without a plan drifts; a plan without a running automation is theatre. Doing both — and calendaring the review — is the difference between having *set up* AI and actually *operating* it.
checklist: Module 04 automation has run at least three times, with Module 05's tool-scope and approval guardrails in place
This month's cost for the automation read off the cost tab and recorded in the plan
One-page operating plan drafted by the grounded assistant and edited to true, with all four sections — Running / Next / Rules / Review
Next section holds exactly two candidates, in priority order, each with a start date
Rules section points at the policy (one line) rather than restating it; policy shared where the team can read it
Roles set so one person besides you can run and check the automation (Member is enough — no full admin needed)
A recurring 15-minute 'AI review' slot exists on a calendar, with its agenda written into the Review section
The whole plan still fits on a single page

resources.title: Automatos docs — Missions
resources.annotation: The shape of the 'Running' automation and the delegation model the plan assembles: the mission lifecycle (goal → plan → approve → run → report), the per-task cost breakdown you read in the review, pause/resume with an increased budget, and 'Save as Routine' to turn a completed mission into a reusable, schedulable playbook. Grounds why Running is only for what's genuinely live.

resources.title: Automatos docs — LLM & Costs
resources.annotation: The cost tab that opens every monthly review: total cost, cost per request, Top Spender, cost by model and by agent, and a projected monthly estimate with anomaly flags. Read it as your 'any surprises?' check. Note honestly what it does NOT provide — no 'hours saved' or ROI figure; that half is your own estimate.

resources.title: Automatos docs — Activity Feed
resources.annotation: The visibility surface behind the review and the Running section: a chronological log of every action and tool call your agents made, so you can confirm the automation ran and read its work back like a manager's weekly skim.

resources.title: Automatos docs — Roles & Permissions
resources.annotation: Backs the 'one person besides you can run and check it' checklist step: three roles — Admin, Member, Viewer. A Member can run playbooks and view activity/reports without full admin; only Admin reaches Settings, credentials, and Team Management. On your own workspace you are the Admin.

resources.title: Automatos docs — About Automatos AI
resources.annotation: The plain-language definitions this module leans on: agents are workers (not chatbots), the platform routes messages for you, knowledge (RAG) powers everything, and visibility is built in. Grounds the 'AI-operational' framing — a running workforce under your rules, not an expert badge.

resources.title: Google Generative AI Leader — exam guide (business-audience credential)
resources.annotation: The honest 'prove it' road named in the send-off: a business-audience AI credential aimed at leaders rather than engineers. The fundamentals and business-strategy sections are vendor-light and match this course's altitude — the credential to reach for if an externally recognised certificate would help you.
